passage_text: When a skateboarder performs a trick, gravity is the force that pulls the board back down to the ground. But gravity also has a curious partner: normal force. When you stand on a ramp, the surface of the ramp pushes back against your feet with a force equal to what gravity is pulling. Scientists call this the normal force because it acts perpendicular — or at a right angle — to the surface. Without this invisible push, your feet would sink right through any surface you stood on. The two forces balance each other, which is why you can stand still on a slope without sliding through the ramp.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word normal most nearly means —

options.text: ordinary or typical
perpendicular to a surface
friendly and easy to get along with
balanced or equal in weight

feedback: The passage defines 'normal' directly: 'perpendicular — or at a right angle — to the surface.' This is the scientific meaning of normal force, not the everyday meaning of ordinary (A). D is tempting because forces are described as balancing, but the passage ties 'normal' to the direction of the force, not its size. C has no support in the text.

passage_text: The ocean is home to an unusual food web in the deep sea. Near hydrothermal vents on the ocean floor, temperatures can reach hundreds of degrees. No sunlight ever reaches these depths, so plants cannot grow there. Instead, bacteria convert the chemicals released by the vents into food — a process called chemosynthesis. These bacteria form the base of the food web. Tiny shrimp graze on the bacteria, and in turn, larger animals consume the shrimp. Scientists were astonished to discover this community because every other food web on Earth was thought to depend on the sun as its primary producer of energy.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word base most nearly means —

options.text: the bottom part of a structure
a military headquarters
the starting point from which all others depend
a chemical that neutralizes acids

feedback: The passage explains that bacteria are the base of the food web — meaning all other organisms depend on them as their source of energy. A (bottom of a structure) is a common meaning and relates loosely to 'floor,' but the passage is talking about dependence in a food chain, not physical position. B and D have no connection to the passage.

passage_text: In chemistry class, students learn that some reactions are reversible. When you dissolve sugar in water, you can evaporate the water and recover the sugar crystals. But other reactions are irreversible — you cannot undo them. When you burn paper, you cannot turn the ash back into paper. Chemists use a special term for reactions that absorb heat from their surroundings: endothermic. You can feel an endothermic reaction happening when you apply a cold pack to a sore muscle. The pack draws heat out of your body, making the area feel cold. The pack itself is the system, and your body is the surroundings that lose energy.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word draws most nearly means —

options.text: sketches a picture
pulls or takes in
ends in a tie
moves toward someone slowly

feedback: The passage says the cold pack 'draws heat out of your body,' meaning it pulls or absorbs heat away. The clue is the phrase 'out of your body,' showing something is being removed. Sketches a picture (A) is the most familiar meaning of 'draws' but does not fit the chemistry context. C (a tie) and D (moving slowly) have no support in the passage.

passage_text: In the 1400s, European nations competed to find faster routes to Asia so they could trade for spices. Portugal sent ships south along the coast of Africa, hoping to round the continent and sail east. Spain decided to sponsor a different voyage: sailing west across the Atlantic. When Columbus landed in the Caribbean in 1492, he believed he had reached Asia. He called the people he met Indians because he thought he was near the Indies. This mix-up had lasting consequences. Europeans rushed to explore and colonize the Americas, which they had not known existed. The exchange of goods, people, and diseases that followed changed both continents forever.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word sponsor most nearly means —

options.text: to put an advertisement on
to support by providing money and resources
to compete against another country
to approve a law in government

feedback: The passage says Spain decided to 'sponsor a different voyage,' which means Spain provided the funding and resources to make the trip possible. The surrounding context — competition among nations and paying for ships — supports the meaning of financial support. A (advertising) is a modern meaning of 'sponsor' but does not fit the historical context. C and D have no support.

passage_text: Maya had been dreading the science fair for weeks. On the morning of the competition, she stood at her display board and watched students with elaborate projects — robots, erupting volcanoes, and glowing chemicals. Her project was simple: she had tested whether plants grew faster with music playing nearby. She had expected to feel embarrassed, but when the judges arrived at her table, something unexpected happened. They leaned in close, asked question after question, and scribbled furiously in their notebooks. One judge told her that her experimental design was the most rigorous he had seen all day. Maya left the gym holding a blue ribbon and a feeling she had never expected — pride.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word elaborate most nearly means —

options.text: carefully explained in detail
highly complex and detailed
expensive to purchase
created by a professional

feedback: Maya compares her simple project to others described as 'elaborate' — robots, erupting volcanoes, and glowing chemicals. These examples show that elaborate means highly complex or detailed, not just expensive or professionally made. A (explained in detail) is another meaning of 'elaborate' used as a verb, but here it is an adjective describing the projects. The contrast with Maya's 'simple' project confirms B.

passage_text: The Silk Road was not a single road but a network of trade routes connecting China, Central Asia, the Middle East, and Europe. Merchants rarely traveled the entire length themselves. Instead, goods changed hands many times, passing from one trader to the next like a relay race. Silk, spices, and precious stones moved west while glass, wool, and gold traveled east. Ideas and religions also traveled these routes. Buddhism spread from India into China along the Silk Road, and Islam expanded westward through the same channels. Historians note that the Silk Road's greatest export may not have been silk at all, but the cultural exchange that reshaped entire civilizations.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word channels most nearly means —

options.text: television stations
deep grooves cut into rock by water
pathways or routes along which things travel
official government organizations

feedback: The passage describes how Islam 'expanded westward through the same channels' as Buddhism. The context — trade routes along which goods and ideas move — tells us channels means pathways or routes. A (TV stations) is the most familiar modern meaning of 'channels' and is a strong distractor. B refers to physical water channels; the Silk Road used land and sea routes, not carved rock. D has no support.

passage_text: The town of Millbrook had one stoplight, one diner, and one unwritten rule: everybody minded their own business. That suited Delia just fine — until the summer Gus Farnsworth moved in next door. Gus had a habit of leaving his radio on the porch at full volume and mowing his grass at six in the morning. Delia tried to ignore it, but after three weeks, her patience had reached its limit. She marched next door and knocked. Gus opened the door looking puzzled, and she explained the situation as calmly as she could. To her shock, Gus apologized immediately and looked genuinely mortified. 'I had no idea,' he said. 'In the city, you never hear anything over the noise.'
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word mortified most nearly means —

options.text: extremely embarrassed and ashamed
angry and defensive
confused about what was being said
hardened or made stiff

feedback: Gus apologizes immediately and says he had no idea he was bothering anyone — showing he feels humbled and ashamed, not angry or confused. 'Genuinely mortified' combined with the apology confirms the meaning of extremely embarrassed. D refers to the biological/scientific meaning of 'mortify' (tissue dying or hardening), which is a strong distractor because it is a real meaning of the word. B and C contradict Gus's behavior in the passage.

passage_text: The settlement of the American West brought conflict between settlers and Native American nations who had lived on the land for thousands of years. The United States government signed dozens of treaties promising Native nations their land would be protected. However, when gold or fertile farmland was discovered, these agreements were routinely broken. Native nations were forced onto smaller and smaller reservations. Leaders such as Sitting Bull and Chief Joseph resisted these removals and became symbols of the struggle to preserve their people's way of life. Historians argue that understanding this history honestly is essential — not to assign blame endlessly, but to acknowledge what happened and why it still matters today.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word reservations most nearly means —

options.text: doubts or concerns about a plan
advance bookings at a hotel or restaurant
areas of land set aside for a specific group
feelings of reluctance before making a decision

feedback: The passage states that Native nations were 'forced onto smaller and smaller reservations,' describing areas of land that the government assigned to them. The context of forced relocation and shrinking land confirms this meaning. A and D are both common meanings of 'reservations' (doubts/reluctance) and are strong distractors. B (restaurant booking) is the most everyday meaning for many students but is clearly not a fit.

passage_text: Rafael had trained for the regional chess tournament every day for six months. He had memorized openings, studied endgames, and practiced against his father until his father refused to play anymore. But in the third round, he faced a girl named Priya who played a style he had never seen — patient, deliberate, and impossible to read. She never seemed rattled. Every time Rafael thought he had seized the advantage, she found a quiet move that neutralized it. He realized, too late, that he had been playing aggressively when he should have been conserving his pieces. The defeat stung, but he recognized that Priya had not beaten him — his own impatience had.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word conserving most nearly means —

options.text: protecting the natural environment
making jam or preserving fruit
carefully preserving and not wasting
storing energy by moving slowly

feedback: Rafael realizes he lost because he did not conserve his pieces — he played aggressively instead of protecting them. The context shows conserving means careful preservation to avoid waste. A (environmental conservation) is a strong distractor because it is the most common association with 'conserving,' but the passage is about chess pieces, not nature. B and D do not fit the chess context.

passage_text: In computer science, an algorithm is a set of step-by-step instructions a computer follows to solve a problem. Think of a recipe: each step must be done in a specific order or the dish will fail. Sorting algorithms help computers organize large amounts of data quickly. One simple sorting algorithm — called bubble sort — works by comparing two items next to each other and swapping them if they are in the wrong order. It then repeats this process across the whole list until no swaps are needed. While bubble sort is easy to understand, it is inefficient for very large data sets. Computer scientists measure an algorithm's efficiency by analyzing how many steps it takes as the amount of data grows.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word analyzing most nearly means —

options.text: breaking something into parts to understand it
completing something carefully and thoroughly
recording data in a table or chart
estimating the cost of a project

feedback: The passage says scientists measure efficiency by 'analyzing how many steps it takes,' meaning they examine and break down the steps to understand performance. The broader context — measuring and comparing — confirms that analyzing means breaking apart to study. B (completing carefully) and D (estimating cost) do not match. C (recording data) is a related but narrower action; the passage is about examination and judgment, not just recording.

passage_text: Marine biologists studying coral reef ecosystems have discovered that reefs function as living cities. Each species occupies a distinct niche: parrotfish grind coral rock into the white sand beaches tourists love, while cleaner wrasse set up stations where larger fish line up to have parasites removed from their scales. The reef's health depends on this intricate web of relationships. When water temperatures rise only two degrees Celsius, corals expel the algae living inside their tissues — a process called bleaching. Without the algae, the coral loses its color and its food source. If temperatures remain elevated, the coral dies, and the city it supports collapses. Scientists emphasize that reefs cover less than one percent of the ocean floor yet support twenty-five percent of all marine species.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word niche most nearly means —

options.text: a small decorative hollow in a wall
a specialized role within a system
a product marketed to a narrow group
an unfilled gap in the marketplace

feedback: The passage describes each species occupying a 'distinct niche' and immediately gives examples of specialized roles — parrotfish grinding coral and wrasse removing parasites. This context makes clear that niche means a specific functional role within an ecosystem. A (wall hollow) is the original architectural meaning and a strong distractor. C and D are modern business meanings also commonly encountered. None of those fit the ecological context established in the passage.

passage_text: When the Constitutional Convention met in Philadelphia in 1787, delegates disagreed sharply about how much power the federal government should hold. Small states feared being dominated by large ones; large states resisted giving smaller states equal say. The solution was a compromise: a bicameral legislature, meaning one with two chambers. In the Senate, every state gets two seats regardless of population. In the House of Representatives, seats are apportioned by population. Critics at the time called this a patchwork solution — sewn together from competing interests rather than built on a single coherent principle. Yet James Madison argued that a government designed to balance competing factions was precisely the point, since no single faction could then seize total control.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word apportioned most nearly means —

options.text: voted for by the general public
divided and distributed according to a formula
assigned randomly by lottery
removed and reduced in number

feedback: The passage contrasts the Senate (equal seats per state) with the House (seats 'apportioned by population'). The contrast makes clear that apportioned means distributed according to a formula — in this case, proportional to each state's population. A (voted for) confuses how seats are filled with how they are allocated. C (lottery) contradicts the population-based formula described. D (removed) is the opposite of distributing seats.

passage_text: Nadia had spent three years building her reputation at the Westfield Debate Club, and she intended to protect it. When the topic for regionals was announced — whether social media helps democracy — she immediately took the affirmative position, confident in her research. But her coach, Mr. Obi, shook his head. 'You're going to argue the negative,' he said. 'Your instinct is always to defend the comfortable position. This will stretch you.' Nadia bristled. She knew the negative case was weak, and she said so. Mr. Obi smiled. 'That's exactly why you need to argue it. The best debaters can make a compelling case for either side. Until you can do that honestly, you haven't mastered the subject — you've only mastered your own bias.'
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word bristled most nearly means —

options.text: stood up straight with confidence
reacted with sharp irritation or defensiveness
became covered with small stiff hairs
spoke loudly to make a point

feedback: After Mr. Obi tells Nadia she'll argue the weak side, she 'bristled' and immediately pushed back — saying the negative case was weak. This shows she reacted with irritation and defensiveness, not confidence or volume. C (hairs standing up) is the literal, physical meaning of 'bristle' and is a strong distractor because it is the most concrete definition. A (standing straight) inverts the emotional signal. D (speaking loudly) is not supported; there is no mention of volume.

passage_text: In mathematics, a function is a rule that assigns exactly one output to each input. Mathematicians describe functions using notation that can look intimidating but is simply a compact language. When a function 'maps' one set of values to another, mathematicians say the first set is the domain and the second is the range. The word 'range' causes confusion because in everyday life it suggests a wide area or a distance that can be covered. In a math function, however, the range is precise and bounded — it is the specific collection of output values the function can actually produce. A function that squares its inputs, for example, can never produce a negative output, so negative numbers are not in its range.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word range most nearly means —

options.text: a wide open area of land used for grazing
the distance over which something can travel
the complete set of output values a function can produce
the difference between the highest and lowest values in a data set

feedback: The passage carefully defines the mathematical meaning of 'range': the specific collection of output values a function can produce. The passage even warns that everyday meanings of 'range' — wide area or distance — cause confusion. D is the statistics definition of 'range' (highest minus lowest), which is another mathematical meaning and the hardest distractor. A (grazing land) and B (distance traveled) are everyday meanings the passage explicitly flags as misleading.

passage_text: When archaeologists excavate an ancient site, they must document every layer of soil before disturbing it. Each layer, called a stratum, represents a different period of time — deeper layers are older. But determining the true age of an artifact requires more than knowing which layer it came from. Artifacts can be intrusive: a Roman coin buried in medieval soil, for example, may have been dropped by a later traveler, not left by the Romans. Archaeologists therefore cross-reference physical evidence with written records, carbon dating, and the company an artifact keeps — meaning the other objects found alongside it. An object in good company tells a more reliable story than one found in isolation.
stem: As used in paragraph 1, the word intrusive most nearly means —

options.text: very large and difficult to miss
out of place in the layer where it was found
rudely interrupting someone else
deliberately hidden to deceive researchers

feedback: The passage illustrates intrusive artifacts with the example of a Roman coin found in medieval soil — something deposited at the wrong time, making it out of place in its layer. The context shows that intrusive means belonging to a different time period than the surrounding stratum. C (rudely interrupting) is the most common everyday meaning of 'intrusive' and is the primary distractor. A (large/visible) and D (deliberately hidden) have no support in the passage.

passage_text: Kenji had practiced his speech for two weeks straight. He memorized every word, every pause, every gesture. On the morning of the competition, his older sister squeezed his shoulder and said, "You've got this in the bag." Kenji took a deep breath and walked onto the stage. When he finished, the audience erupted in applause. His sister had been right — all those hours of practice had paid off. He walked back to his seat feeling like he was standing ten feet tall.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "You've got this in the bag." What does this idiom mean?

options.text: You are carrying a bag with your notes inside it.
You will definitely succeed at this.
You have hidden something important from the judges.
You are nervous and trying to hide it.

feedback: "Got this in the bag" is an idiom meaning something is already secured or certain to succeed. The context clues — two weeks of practice, every gesture memorized, and the sister's encouraging tone — confirm she is predicting Kenji will win, not commenting on a physical bag. Option A is the literal distractor. Option C and D misread the encouraging tone of the idiom.

passage_text: The old lighthouse stood at the edge of the rocky cliff. Every night, its beam swept across the dark water like a giant finger pointing the way home. Sailors who had been lost in storms said the light felt like a friend calling out to them through the darkness. The lighthouse keeper polished the lens each morning, knowing that tiny task kept ships safe. Without that steady, patient light, the coast would be a much more dangerous place.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "its beam swept across the dark water like a giant finger pointing the way home." What does this simile mean?

options.text: The lighthouse beam was shaped exactly like a human finger.
The beam moved slowly and was difficult to see.
The beam moved in a sweeping motion and guided sailors toward safety.
The lighthouse keeper used his finger to direct passing ships.

feedback: The simile compares the lighthouse beam to a giant finger pointing home, emphasizing how the beam sweeps in a directed motion and guides lost sailors. Context clues — sailors lost in storms, the light feeling like "a friend calling out" — reinforce the guidance meaning. Option A is the literal distractor (no actual finger). Option D confuses the tenor and vehicle. Option B contradicts the passage, which shows the light is effective and steady.

passage_text: The science fair judges walked from table to table, asking questions and taking notes. Maya watched them pause at her volcano display and whisper to each other. Her heart was a drum beating faster and faster as they approached. They asked her three questions. She answered each one clearly, remembering everything her dad had helped her rehearse. When the judges moved on, Maya exhaled slowly. Whatever happened next, she had done her best.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "Her heart was a drum beating faster and faster as they approached." What does this metaphor mean?

options.text: Maya's heart was shaped like a drum inside her chest.
Maya could hear drumming music playing in the background.
Maya felt an intense, rapid heartbeat because she was nervous and excited.
Maya was so calm that her heart barely moved.

feedback: The metaphor equates Maya's heart to a drum, and "beating faster and faster" conveys intensifying nervousness as the judges approach. Context clues — the judges whispering, Maya watching anxiously, rehearsed answers — all establish anxiety and anticipation. Option A is the literal distractor. Option B invents external music not in the passage. Option D contradicts the increasing rhythm described.

passage_text: The twins had argued about everything since kindergarten — whose turn it was to sit in front, who got the last piece of bread, even which direction to walk to school. But when their dog Pepper went missing, they forgot every disagreement. They searched the whole neighborhood together, calling his name until their voices were gone. When they finally found Pepper under the porch, Marcus looked at his sister and said, "Let's bury the hatchet — at least until tomorrow." They both laughed.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "Let's bury the hatchet — at least until tomorrow." What does this idiom mean?

options.text: Let's hide our tools in the backyard.
Let's agree to stop fighting and make peace with each other.
Let's dig in the dirt to find something we lost.
Let's stay mad at each other for just one more day.

feedback: "Bury the hatchet" is an idiom meaning to end a conflict and make peace. The context — the twins' long history of arguing, their cooperation during the search, and the humor of "at least until tomorrow" — confirms Marcus is proposing a truce. Option A is the literal distractor (burying an actual tool). Option C misreads the idiom. Option D inverts the meaning by taking "tomorrow" as the main idea rather than Marcus's wry joke about the truce.

passage_text: The wind rattled the shutters and hissed through the gaps around the door all night. By morning, the yard was a mess of fallen branches. Grandma stood at the window with her coffee and shook her head. "That storm sure threw a tantrum," she said. She pulled on her boots and went outside to start clearing branches. The air smelled clean and sharp after all the rain, and the sun was already breaking through the last gray clouds.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "That storm sure threw a tantrum." What does this personification mean?

options.text: The storm was weak and did very little damage.
A child threw objects during the storm.
The storm behaved like an angry person, acting out violently and making a mess.
Grandma was angry about having to clean up the yard.

feedback: Personification gives the storm a human behavior — throwing a tantrum — to convey that it acted with wild, destructive energy like an angry child. Context clues — rattling shutters, hissing wind, fallen branches, the mess in the yard — support the idea of violent, chaotic behavior. Option A contradicts the described damage. Option B is the literal distractor (a real child). Option D misattributes the feeling to Grandma rather than the storm.

passage_text: Coach Rivera stood at the whiteboard during halftime, her marker squeaking across the surface as she drew arrows and circles. The team had been playing sloppy — missed passes, lazy defense, two completely avoidable penalties. "Here's what I see," she said, pointing to the left side of the field. "We are leaving the door wide open over there, and they are walking right through it." She capped her marker. "That stops now." The team nodded. Nobody wanted to be the one who had left the door open.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "We are leaving the door wide open over there, and they are walking right through it." What does this idiom mean?

options.text: The team forgot to close the gate at the edge of the field.
The team is inviting the other team to join their side.
The team is creating a weakness on one side that the opponents are successfully exploiting.
The team is playing too aggressively and running through the other team's defense.

feedback: "Leaving the door wide open" is an idiom for creating a vulnerability or gap that opponents can take advantage of. Coach Rivera draws this on the whiteboard while discussing a specific side of the field, and the context of missed passes and penalties confirms she means a defensive breakdown being exploited. Option A is the literal distractor (an actual door). Option B misreads "open" as welcoming rather than vulnerable. Option D inverts the direction — it's the opponents who are exploiting the gap, not the team being too aggressive.

passage_text: Preparing for the statewide robotics competition was like building a sandcastle in a rising tide. Every time the team solved one problem — the gripper arm, the sensor calibration, the code loop — two more would surface. Priya stayed late every Thursday, reworking the program while the others went home. She knew they were close, but "close" kept moving further away. By the final week, the team had rewritten their code seven times. Priya didn't complain. She just kept building.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "Preparing for the statewide robotics competition was like building a sandcastle in a rising tide." What does this simile mean?

options.text: The team practiced on a beach where the tide kept interrupting them.
The team's preparation felt hopeless because the challenges kept washing away their progress as fast as they made it.
The team was building something fragile that was beautiful but would not last long.
The team enjoyed the preparation process the way children enjoy building at the beach.

feedback: The simile compares preparation to building a sandcastle in rising water — each time something is built, the tide erases it. The passage supports this with the pattern of solving one problem only to have two more appear, and "close" kept moving further away. Option A is the literal distractor (an actual beach). Option C captures fragility but misses the key idea of continuous erasure of progress. Option D inverts the emotional tone — the passage describes frustrating hard work, not enjoyment.

passage_text: The town council had argued about the new highway route for three years. Some members wanted it to cut through the eastern wetlands to save money. Others insisted the wetlands had to be protected. Finally, after months of deadlock, Councilwoman Patel announced she had found a third route that satisfied both sides. "I think we can finally close the book on this one," she said at the press conference, smoothing her notes. The room went quiet for a moment, then broke into tired, relieved applause.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "I think we can finally close the book on this one." What does this idiom mean?

options.text: The council will put away the documents about the highway.
The council needs to read more information before making a decision.
This long, unresolved dispute can now be considered finished and settled.
The councilwoman is frustrated and refuses to discuss the issue any further.

feedback: "Close the book on" is an idiom meaning to bring something to a complete and final conclusion. Three years of argument and a deadlock make the context of a long-running unresolved dispute clear, and finding a third route that satisfies both sides is what enables closure. Option A is the literal distractor (physically filing papers). Option B inverts the meaning — more reading would keep the book open. Option D misreads the tone; the relieved applause and satisfied smile indicate resolution, not frustration.

passage_text: The empty factory had sat at the edge of town for eleven years. Its broken windows stared out at the road like hollow eyes, and rust crept up the walls the way ivy climbs a garden fence. Weeds had swallowed the parking lot. Sometimes kids dared each other to walk up to the door, but no one ever went inside. The building seemed to hold its breath — waiting for something, though no one could say what. Last spring, a developer finally bought the lot and promised to tear it all down.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "The building seemed to hold its breath — waiting for something, though no one could say what." What does this personification mean?

options.text: The building had an air filtration system that was no longer working.
The building appeared eerily still and suspended in time, as if anticipating an unknown change.
The building was so fragile that any movement might cause it to collapse.
The building was releasing gases that made the air around it dangerous to breathe.

feedback: Personification gives the building a human action — holding its breath — to convey a sense of frozen, tense anticipation. The passage builds this feeling with eleven years of emptiness, the eerie detail of no one daring to enter, and the eventual arrival of a developer signaling change. Option A is the literal distractor (actual ventilation). Option C focuses on structural fragility, which is a different idea not supported by the breath imagery. Option D invents a physical hazard not mentioned in the passage.

passage_text: The new student, Amara, had transferred from three schools in two years. She had learned not to unpack her feelings the way other kids unpacked their backpacks — quickly, carelessly, leaving everything out on display. Instead, she kept them zipped up tight. At Jefferson Middle, she watched from a distance for two weeks before deciding that maybe — maybe — this place was worth the risk of opening up. She started sitting with a group at lunch. She even laughed out loud once, and didn't immediately feel embarrassed about it.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "She had learned not to unpack her feelings the way other kids unpacked their backpacks — quickly, carelessly, leaving everything out on display." What does this metaphor mean?

options.text: Amara had lost her backpack too many times and stopped bringing one to school.
Amara was disorganized and often forgot her school supplies.
Amara had learned to guard her emotions carefully rather than sharing them openly and without hesitation.
Amara expressed her feelings loudly and dramatically, the way children dump out a backpack.

feedback: The metaphor compares unpacking feelings to unpacking a backpack, and the contrast is between doing it carelessly (leaving everything on display) versus keeping it "zipped up tight." Amara's history of transferring schools explains why she guards herself. Option A is the literal distractor (actual backpacks). Option B misapplies the metaphor to organizational habits, not emotional ones. Option D inverts the meaning — Amara explicitly does NOT unpack carelessly; she keeps things zipped.

passage_text: By the time the investigation reached its third month, Detective Osei had interviewed forty-seven witnesses, reviewed six thousand pages of financial records, and barely slept. His partner kept suggesting they hand the case to another department. "Let someone else carry this water," she said one morning over cold coffee. Osei shook his head. He had started something that mattered — a fraud scheme that had cheated hundreds of ordinary families — and walking away felt unthinkable. He would carry the water as long as it needed carrying.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "Let someone else carry this water." What does this idiom mean?

options.text: Let another detective bring beverages to the team during long shifts.
Let another department take on the difficult, exhausting burden of this investigation.
Let someone with more experience solve the case quickly and efficiently.
Let another detective expose the truth that Osei is hiding about the case.

feedback: "Carry this water" is an idiom for doing the difficult, unglamorous work of bearing a heavy responsibility. Osei's partner is suggesting they offload the exhausting burden of the case — three months of grueling work, 47 witnesses, 6,000 pages of records, no sleep. Option A is the literal distractor (actual water/drinks). Option C introduces the idea of expertise, which the passage does not suggest — the partner wants to quit, not find a better person. Option D introduces a hidden-truth angle that has no support in the passage.

passage_text: The river had always been the town's quiet conscience. It remembered every drought and every flood, every generation of children who had waded its shallows and every farmer who had cursed its rising banks. When the chemical plant moved upstream, the river did not protest — it simply changed color. First a dull brown, then a greenish gray that caught the light strangely in the afternoon. The town watched but did not act. Years later, when the fish were gone and the wells ran bitter, the river was still there, keeping its patient, silent record.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "The river had always been the town's quiet conscience." What does this metaphor mean?

options.text: The river was so shallow and calm that it barely made any sound as it flowed.
The river silently recorded and reflected the town's choices and their consequences, like a moral witness.
The river was polluted so severely that it could no longer support any living creatures.
The river is personified as a living creature that felt guilt about the town's suffering.

feedback: The metaphor calls the river the town's "quiet conscience" — a conscience being the inner moral awareness that records right and wrong. The passage develops this with the river "remembering" generations, changing color silently when polluted, and "keeping its patient, silent record" of the town's inaction. Option A is the literal distractor (a quiet, calm river). Option C is a true fact from the passage (the fish died) but describes an event, not what the metaphor means. Option D confuses metaphor with personification — the question asks what the conscience metaphor means, not whether other figurative devices are present.

passage_text: Nadia had spent her whole life being the supporting wall — the one who held everything up while everyone else built something on top of her. She helped her younger brothers with homework while her own assignments waited. She covered shifts at her mother's bakery without being asked. She never complained, and people rarely noticed. When her school counselor asked what Nadia wanted for herself, Nadia went quiet for a long time. It had never occurred to her that she was allowed to want anything. She was the wall. Walls don't want things.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "She had spent her whole life being the supporting wall — the one who held everything up while everyone else built something on top of her." What does this metaphor mean?

options.text: Nadia worked in construction and helped build structures for her family.
Nadia sacrificed her own desires and always provided the invisible, essential support that allowed others to pursue their goals.
Nadia was so strong and silent that nobody at school noticed she existed.
Nadia admired people who were strong and wished she could be more like them.

feedback: The extended metaphor of a supporting wall emphasizes invisible, structural sacrifice — Nadia holds others up without being seen or credited. Context: covering shifts unprompted, doing brothers' homework while her own waited, never complaining, nobody noticing. The counselor's question and Nadia's silence confirm she has never considered her own desires. Option A is the literal distractor (construction work). Option C focuses only on invisibility and drops the self-sacrifice element central to the metaphor. Option D inverts the direction — Nadia is the wall herself; she isn't admiring others for that quality.

passage_text: The archive room in the university library had not been properly organized in decades. Boxes were piled to the ceiling in no particular order, and the catalog cards were older than most of the professors. When the new archivist, Dr. Fenwick, arrived, she stood in the doorway and assessed the situation. "This," she told her assistant, "is a project that will eat us alive if we let it." She did not let it. Over the next two years, she worked methodically, box by box, never skipping ahead, never panicking.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "This is a project that will eat us alive if we let it." What does this idiom mean?

options.text: The archive boxes contain materials that are dangerous to handle without protective equipment.
The project will become so overwhelming that it will consume all of their time and energy and defeat them if they lose control of it.
The project is so interesting that Dr. Fenwick and her assistant will become obsessed with it.
The project will generate so much public attention that it will make Dr. Fenwick famous.

feedback: "Eat us alive" is an idiom meaning to overwhelm and destroy one's ability to cope or succeed. Dr. Fenwick's careful, methodical response — working box by box, never panicking — shows she is actively preventing the project from doing exactly that. The scope (decades of disorganization, ceiling-high boxes) establishes the threat. Option A is the literal distractor (physical danger from the materials). Option C misreads the idiom as expressing obsession rather than being overwhelmed. Option D introduces public fame, which has no support in the passage.

passage_text: The old oak at the center of the schoolyard had watched four generations of students scratch their initials into its bark. It had sheltered first kisses, witnessed fistfights, and absorbed the sound of a hundred graduation speeches through its deep, patient wood. When the school board voted to cut it down for a new parking structure, the students organized a protest. They chained themselves to the trunk. The tree, for its part, simply stood — as it always had — neither asking for rescue nor expecting it, indifferent to the fuss the way only very old things can be.
stem: Read this sentence from the passage: "The tree, for its part, simply stood — as it always had — neither asking for rescue nor expecting it, indifferent to the fuss the way only very old things can be." What does this personification mean?

options.text: The tree was dead and no longer responded to the environment around it.
The tree had already been cut down by the time students arrived to protest.
The tree is given a quality of calm, ancient detachment — as if its long existence placed it beyond the urgency of human disputes.
The tree is personified as grateful that the students are fighting to save it.

feedback: The personification attributes to the tree a human emotional stance — indifference, lack of expectation, neither asking nor hoping — and ties it to the tree's extreme age. The passage frames this as a quality unique to "very old things," elevating the tree above the human urgency of the protest. Option A is the literal distractor (a dead tree). Option B contradicts the passage (the students chained themselves to it; it was still standing). Option D inverts the emotional quality — the passage explicitly says the tree was not expecting rescue or grateful, which would be the opposite of the described indifference.

passage_text: A microscope changed everything for Dr. Yemi. With the naked eye, she could only see that the pond water looked murky. But under the microscope, an entire world came into view — single-celled creatures no bigger than a grain of dust glided past, spun in tight circles, and bumped into one another. Her students crowded around the eyepiece, each taking a turn. 'You're looking at organisms that are far too small to see without help,' Dr. Yemi told them. 'That is exactly what the tool's name tells you: it makes tiny things visible.' The students nodded and reached for their lab notebooks to record what they saw.
stem: Dr. Yemi says the microscope's name tells you what it does. Based on the word parts micro- and -scope, what does a microscope most likely do?

options.text: It projects images onto a large screen for a group to see.
It makes very small objects visible by magnifying them.
It measures the exact size of objects in millimeters.
It records living organisms so they can be studied later.

feedback: Micro- means 'small' and -scope means 'to look at or observe.' The passage confirms this: Dr. Yemi says the tool makes 'tiny things visible.' Choice A describes a projector, C describes a measuring instrument, and D describes a recorder — none of those match what the word parts or the passage describe.

passage_text: The geology field trip took the class to a rocky hillside where layers of stone lay exposed like the pages of a very old book. Their guide, Mr. Obasi, crouched beside a gray slab and ran his finger along a dark stripe. 'This stripe is charcoal from an ancient fire,' he said. 'And this study — geology — is named for exactly what we are examining right now.' He pointed to the hillside and waited. One student raised her hand: 'Geo- means Earth.' Mr. Obasi smiled. 'Exactly. And -logy means the study of. So geology is the study of Earth — its rocks, its layers, its history written in stone.' The class took photos and began sketching the layers in their field journals.
stem: Mr. Obasi explains what 'geology' means by breaking the word apart. Based on the word parts geo- and -logy, what does geology mean?

options.text: The study of living creatures found underground
The measurement of distances across Earth's surface
The study of Earth, including its rocks and layers
The history of how humans have changed the land

feedback: Geo- means 'Earth' and -logy means 'the study of.' The passage confirms this directly: Mr. Obasi says 'geology is the study of Earth — its rocks, its layers, its history written in stone.' Choice A adds 'living creatures,' which is biology, not geology. B describes measuring distances, and D focuses on human history — neither matches the word parts or the passage.

passage_text: When the marine biologist Ms. Forde described her research vessel to the class, she saved the best part for last. 'We lower a thermometer into the water at different depths,' she said, 'but for mapping the temperature of an entire ocean region, we use a thermograph. The graph is what makes it special — it doesn't just take a reading at one moment. It draws out a continuous record over time, tracing how the temperature rises and falls as we sail.' She paused while students copied the word into their notes. 'Think carefully about both parts of the word,' she added. 'Therm- alone can mean heat in general — but what does the -graph part add that a plain thermometer cannot give you?'
stem: Ms. Forde asks what the -graph part adds to the word 'thermograph.' Based on the word parts therm- and -graph, what does a thermograph do that a plain thermometer does NOT?

options.text: It measures a wider range of temperatures than a thermometer can.
It produces a written or drawn record of temperature changes over time.
It converts heat into a visible color so the reading is easier to see.
It warns researchers when the water temperature becomes dangerous.

feedback: Therm- means 'heat' and -graph means 'write' or 'draw a record.' The passage confirms this contrast: Ms. Forde says the thermograph 'draws out a continuous record over time,' while a plain thermometer only gives a single reading. Choice A is about range, C is about color conversion, and D is about warnings — none of those are supported by the word parts or the passage.

passage_text: Before the invention of the telephone, people used a telescope to watch ships approach the harbor from a great distance and signal the docks early. The harbor master's apprentice, a girl named Sable, had learned to use the instrument when she was nine. She held it steady against the window ledge and peered through the eyepiece. 'Tele- means far,' her uncle had taught her, 'and -scope means to observe. So this device observes things that are far away.' Sable could tell from the flag pattern which ship it was, but she couldn't tell its cargo. A colleague had suggested using a newer device — a hydrophone — to listen for sounds traveling through the water. That, Sable thought, was something her telescope would never manage.
stem: Sable's uncle explains the parts of the word 'telescope.' Based on the word parts tele- and -scope, what does a telescope most likely do?

options.text: It captures and records sounds from distant locations.
It allows a person to observe things that are far away.
It measures the distance between two far-apart points.
It transmits messages across long distances using light signals.

feedback: Tele- means 'far' or 'at a distance' and -scope means 'to observe.' The passage confirms this: Sable's uncle says 'this device observes things that are far away,' and the passage shows Sable using it to watch ships approach from a great distance. Choice A describes a device that records sound. C describes a measuring device. D describes a signaling device. None of those match both the word parts and the passage.

passage_text: At the science fair, twelve-year-old Priya displayed a project she had titled 'Photovoltaic Cells and the Future of Energy.' Her poster explained that photo- comes from the Greek word for light and that -volt refers to the unit of electrical force. Several judges stopped at her table. One asked, 'Could you have called your project photosynthesis instead?' Priya shook her head. 'Photosynthesis means putting things together using light — that's what plants do. My cells don't build anything. They convert.' A second judge pointed to a separate panel: 'So photovoltaic means converting light into voltage — into electrical force?' Priya nodded. 'The word tells you exactly what happens: light goes in, electrical force comes out.' The judges marked their rubrics and moved on.
stem: The judge asks whether 'photosynthesis' and 'photovoltaic' could be used interchangeably. Based on the shared word part photo- and the different second parts (-synthesis vs. -voltaic), why are they NOT the same?

options.text: Photo- means different things in each word: 'heat' in photosynthesis and 'light' in photovoltaic.
Both use light, but -synthesis means building or combining, while -voltaic relates to electrical force — so the processes they describe are different.
Photosynthesis requires sunlight outdoors, but photovoltaic cells work only indoors under artificial light.
They are interchangeable; both words describe the same process of converting light to energy.

feedback: Photo- means 'light' in both words — so A is wrong. The difference lies in the second part: -synthesis means 'putting together or combining' (plants build sugars), while -voltaic relates to voltage or electrical force (solar cells produce electricity). Priya makes this exact distinction: 'Photosynthesis means putting things together using light... My cells don't build anything. They convert.' C and D are not supported by the passage or the word parts.

passage_text: After the long hike up the mountain trail, Marcus trudged the final half mile back to camp. His boots were soaked, his pack felt heavier than ever, and each step took real effort to push forward. His friend Deja skipped ahead, full of energy and laughing at the clouds. Marcus didn't bother calling out to her. He focused on putting one foot in front of the other, dragging himself along the muddy path until the tents finally came into view. When he reached camp, he dropped his pack, sat on a log, and didn't move for ten minutes. 'That last stretch was brutal,' he said.
stem: In this passage, Marcus 'trudged' the final half mile. Which word could BEST replace 'trudged' while keeping the meaning the same?

options.text: strolled
sprinted
plodded
wandered

feedback: The passage shows Marcus exhausted, boots soaked, pack heavy, taking each step with effort — this is labored, heavy walking. 'Plodded' means to walk heavily and slowly with great effort, which matches the passage exactly. 'Strolled' suggests a relaxed, leisurely walk — the opposite of what Marcus is doing. 'Sprinted' means running fast. 'Wandered' means walking without a set direction. Neither of those fits the context.

passage_text: Nadia glanced at the math problem on the board and quickly looked away. She had seen enough — a fraction divided by a fraction with different denominators. She already knew she didn't understand it. Across the room, her classmate Devon stared at the same problem, leaning forward slightly, his eyes fixed on each number and symbol. He turned over the problem in his mind, moving his pencil in the air without touching the page. The two students could not have been more different: one barely noticed the question was there; the other could not stop examining every part of it.
stem: In this passage, Devon 'stared' at the math problem. Which word could BEST replace 'stared' while keeping the meaning the same?

options.text: glanced
gazed
glimpsed
peered

feedback: The passage describes Devon leaning forward, eyes fixed on the problem, examining every number carefully — this is a long, steady, concentrated look. 'Gazed' means to look steadily and intently at something, which matches Devon's focused attention. 'Glanced' is already used in the passage to describe Nadia's brief, inattentive look — the opposite of Devon. 'Glimpsed' also means a brief, partial look. 'Peered' suggests squinting or straining to see something unclear, but the problem on the board is visible — Devon just can't stop studying it.

passage_text: Coach Rivas watched the two runners warm up before the race. The first runner, Tomás, strode onto the track with long, powerful steps, arms swinging, chin up. He moved like someone who had somewhere important to be and intended to get there fast. The second runner, Isabel, strolled along the outer lane, her pace unhurried, pausing to stretch one calf against the railing and check her watch. Coach Rivas made a note: Tomás was already in race mode; Isabel was still conserving every drop of energy she had. Both approaches worked. Their styles before a race were simply different.
stem: In this passage, Tomás 'strode' onto the track. Which word could BEST replace 'strode' while keeping the meaning the same?

options.text: shuffled
marched
ambled
crept

feedback: The passage shows Tomás moving with long, powerful steps, arms swinging, chin up — purposeful and energetic. 'Marched' means to walk with deliberate, energetic strides, which matches this description closely. 'Shuffled' means to drag the feet in a slow, tired way — the opposite of Tomás. 'Ambled' means to walk slowly and leisurely, which better fits Isabel. 'Crept' means to move very slowly and quietly. Neither of those matches Tomás's purposeful, powerful walk.

passage_text: From behind the curtain, Lily peered at the audience filling the auditorium seats. She couldn't see their faces clearly — the stage lights were too bright and the house lights too dim — but she could make out a sea of shapes settling in. Somewhere out there, she knew, her parents were sitting in the third row. She searched for them, squinting through the narrow gap in the curtain, trying to make out a familiar silhouette. Her heart pounded. It was only when the piano introduction began that she finally let the curtain fall and took her place at center stage.
stem: In this passage, Lily 'peered' at the audience from behind the curtain. Which word could BEST replace 'peered' while keeping the meaning the same?

options.text: admired
gawked
squinted
glared

feedback: The passage shows Lily looking through a narrow gap, in poor light, straining to make out shapes and silhouettes — this is effortful looking under difficult conditions. 'Squinted' means to look with eyes partially closed in order to see better, which matches the difficulty of looking through a narrow curtain gap in dim light. 'Admired' means to look with appreciation or pleasure — Lily isn't admiring the audience, she's searching nervously. 'Gawked' means to stare rudely in an open-mouthed way. 'Glared' means to stare angrily. Neither of those fits the nervous, straining quality of Lily's look.

passage_text: The detective examined the study methodically. She didn't rush across the room — she inched along the walls, pausing at each object, tilting her head, and then moving on. She scrutinized the spines of every book on the shelf, noting which ones were dusty and which had been recently handled. She ran a gloved finger along the edge of the desk without disturbing anything. Her partner, who had finished in five minutes, leaned in the doorway. 'Find anything?' he asked. 'Not yet,' she said, still not turning around. 'But I haven't looked at everything.' She would not rush. There was too much to see.
stem: In this passage, the detective 'scrutinized' the spines of every book. Which word could BEST replace 'scrutinized' while keeping the meaning the same?

options.text: skimmed
regarded
inspected
noticed

feedback: The passage shows the detective moving slowly and deliberately, pausing at each object, noting specific details like which books are dusty and which are not — this is careful, thorough examination. 'Inspected' means to examine something closely and carefully for a specific purpose, which matches the detective's methodical scrutiny. 'Skimmed' means to look over quickly and superficially — the opposite of what the passage describes. 'Regarded' means simply to look at or consider, with no implication of close examination. 'Noticed' means to become aware of something without implying any deliberate, close inspection.

passage_text: Mrs. Okoro always knew exactly how much things cost. When the class planned a pizza party, she checked three different stores and chose the brand that gave the most slices per dollar. When her family went on vacation, she compared hotels until she found one with a pool that cost half what the others charged. Her students joked that she could stretch a dollar into ten. Her daughter called her thrifty, and meant it as a compliment — someone who wastes nothing and values every cent. Mrs. Okoro just smiled. 'I call it smart,' she said.
stem: The passage says Mrs. Okoro's daughter called her 'thrifty.' What does this word suggest about how the daughter views Mrs. Okoro's money habits?

options.text: The daughter thinks Mrs. Okoro is selfish for never sharing her money with others.
The daughter views Mrs. Okoro's careful spending as a positive and admirable quality.
The daughter believes Mrs. Okoro is too worried about money to enjoy herself.
The daughter thinks Mrs. Okoro is embarrassingly cheap and should spend more freely.

feedback: 'Thrifty' carries a positive connotation — it means using money carefully and wisely, without waste. The passage confirms this: the daughter 'meant it as a compliment.' Mrs. Okoro's behavior (comparing prices, finding the best value) is presented as skilled and admirable, not selfish or excessive. Choices A, C, and D all imply a negative or critical view, which contradicts both the word's connotation and the passage's context.

passage_text: Everyone on the team knew not to argue with Brianna once she had made up her mind. During the science fair project, when the group wanted to change the experiment, Brianna just shook her head. 'We already decided,' she said, and that was the end of it. She wouldn't hear new ideas, wouldn't look at anyone else's data, and blocked every attempt to change the plan. Her teammates called her stubborn behind her back. They liked Brianna, but working with her when she'd dug in her heels was exhausting. They spent more time fighting the old plan than building the new one.
stem: The passage says Brianna's teammates called her 'stubborn.' What does this word suggest about how the teammates feel about Brianna's refusal to change?

options.text: They think Brianna's commitment to the plan is an admirable sign of leadership.
They are frustrated and view her refusal to change as an unreasonable obstacle.
They feel grateful that Brianna keeps the group focused on the original goal.
They believe Brianna's approach will ultimately produce the best science fair result.

feedback: 'Stubborn' carries a negative connotation — it describes someone who refuses to change in a way that others find unreasonable and frustrating. The passage confirms this: the teammates 'called her stubborn behind her back,' working with her was 'exhausting,' and they 'spent more time fighting the old plan than building the new one.' Choices A, C, and D all imply a positive view, but the word 'stubborn' and the passage context both signal frustration, not admiration.

passage_text: Two food bloggers reviewed the same new restaurant on the same night. The first wrote: 'The portions were economical — the kitchen clearly measured every ingredient with care, giving each dish exactly what it needed and no more.' The second wrote: 'The portions were stingy — we left still hungry, and the prices were far too high for what arrived on the plate.' Both bloggers had the same meal. But the word each chose to describe the portion size revealed exactly how each felt about leaving the table.
stem: The first blogger uses the word 'economical' and the second blogger uses the word 'stingy' to describe the same portion size. What is the MOST important difference between these two words?

options.text: 'Economical' suggests the portions were just right and well-managed; 'stingy' suggests the portions were unreasonably small and disappointing.
'Economical' means the food was inexpensive; 'stingy' means the food was overpriced.
Both words describe the same attitude toward the restaurant — mild disappointment.
'Economical' is a more formal word than 'stingy,' but they carry the same meaning.

feedback: Both words can describe small or careful portions, but their connotations are very different. 'Economical' is neutral to slightly positive — it suggests careful, efficient use of resources without waste. 'Stingy' is negative — it implies a meanness that leaves someone feeling cheated or unsatisfied. The passage makes this contrast explicit: the first blogger praises the kitchen's care; the second complains about leaving hungry. Choice B confuses connotation with price. C is wrong — the two words express opposite attitudes. D is wrong — the words carry meaningfully different connotations, not just different registers.

passage_text: The candidate for class president had a reputation for never giving up. During the campaign, she knocked on every classroom door, revised her speech four times, and stayed after school to hang posters when everyone else had gone home. Some students admired her and called her persistent — someone who keeps going with purpose and belief in the goal. Others found her tiresome and called her relentless — someone who never stops even when it would be reasonable to ease up. The votes came in close. She won by a margin of eight.
stem: Some students called the candidate 'persistent' and others called her 'relentless.' What is the MOST important difference in how these two words describe the same quality?

options.text: 'Persistent' suggests admirable determination that keeps going toward a goal; 'relentless' suggests an exhausting refusal to stop that can wear others out.
'Persistent' means working slowly over a long time; 'relentless' means working quickly without pausing.
Both words carry a positive connotation; 'relentless' is just a stronger form of 'persistent.'
'Persistent' describes physical effort; 'relentless' describes mental determination.

feedback: Both words describe someone who keeps going, but their connotations differ. 'Persistent' is positive — the passage pairs it with students who admired her and links it to purpose and belief. 'Relentless' carries a harsher edge — the passage pairs it with students who found her tiresome and suggests she doesn't stop 'even when it would be reasonable to ease up.' The same behavior, described with two different words, reveals two different emotional responses to it. Choice B confuses speed with endurance. C is wrong — 'relentless' is not simply a stronger positive. D introduces a physical/mental split that the passage does not support.

passage_text: The biography described how the city's founder, Governor Harwick, had handled every decision alone. He signed contracts without consulting the council. He redirected the new road without asking the property owners along the route. He replaced the bridge designer three days before construction began. His supporters called him decisive — a leader who cuts through delay and acts when action is needed. His critics called him impulsive — someone who leaps before looking and lets confidence substitute for judgment. The historian writing the biography declined to choose a side. She noted only that Harwick's bridge stood for two hundred years, and that the road he rerouted flooded every spring.
stem: Harwick's supporters called him 'decisive' and his critics called him 'impulsive.' The historian does not choose either word. What does the historian's choice suggest about the difference between 'decisive' and 'impulsive'?

options.text: The historian thinks 'decisive' is accurate because the bridge lasted two hundred years, proving Harwick made good decisions.
The historian thinks 'impulsive' is accurate because the road flooded every spring, proving Harwick made bad decisions.

By presenting mixed evidence, the historian shows that 'decisive' and 'impulsive' are not objective descriptions — they are connotative judgments that reflect how the speaker feels about the same behavior.
The historian avoids both words because they are too informal for a biography.

feedback: The historian presents evidence that neither fully supports nor fully rejects Harwick's method: the bridge stood for two centuries (a success), but the road flooded every spring (a failure). By refusing to choose 'decisive' or 'impulsive,' the historian implies that both words describe the same underlying behavior — acting alone, quickly, without consultation — but that the word chosen reveals the speaker's attitude rather than an objective fact. 'Decisive' is positive; 'impulsive' is negative. This is the essence of connotation. Choices A and B each pick a side the historian deliberately avoids. D misreads the reason for the historian's neutrality — the issue is not formality but conflicting evidence.
